text: Welcome to This is My Architecture.
I'm Danny and I'm joined by Michel from Mimic.
Today we're going to be talking about Edge Engine.
Thank you for joining us today.
Hey Danny, how are you?
Doing great.
Can you tell us a little bit about what Edge Engine is?
So Edge Engine is a technology which allows developers to design,
develop, and deploy Edge microservice on nodes like Android and iOS.
So what would you build on top of this?
Can you give me a sample application?
So an example would be an oil industry type scenario
where a smart pump will basically have problems
and will be when the field agent arrives with an Android tablet,
will be able to directly talk to the smart pump
instead of having to go through the cloud in order to get information.
Okay.
And so how do we enable that type of connectivity?
So how it works is we have nodes which run this Edge Engine
and when the first node arrives on the network,
it basically registers to the AKS and something called MDS,
which is a discovery service, and this becomes then a supernode.
When a new node arrives on the network, it listens to the supernode,
and when this listening happens, then supernode will register this new node on MDS.
Okay. And when that data is registered, where does it get stored?
It gets stored in MongoDB because we use a document database for that.
And why did you choose MongoDB for that?
So we use MongoDB because one of the features we have on top of the Edge Service Mesh by network
is an Edge Service Mesh by proximity.
So we have another service node somewhere else but close.
MongoDB, because this one registered like that,
MongoDB will be used in order to define a cluster by proximity.
Oh, excellent. And so what is DynamoDB used for?
So DynamoDB is used because we have other services like profile,
like association, hierarchical association by peer,
because we have to develop a bunch of services which basically are knowledgeable
of the fact that there is this service mesh and there is this ability to have Edge microservice,
because sometimes even a service like a profile will need to download an Edge microservice on node.
Okay. And can you monitor this Edge network?
Yes. Supernode reports data information like IPI logs or business logic and things like this
to Kinesis data stream which then pushes the data to the firehose to S3 in order to archive the information.
Okay. And then?
And then we have Snowflake which basically will create information from S3 in order to do SQL type query.
And so from that you can get real-time analytics of what's going on in your network?
So we get either real-time analytics or long-term analytics because we retain actually very few information in Snowflake.
All the information stays on S3 and depending on the usage, we either interrogate quickly in order to have real-time
or we interrogate over a month period in order to have more long-term analytics.
Can you tell me a little bit about what the sense of scale of the system is? How many messages that you're processing?
So the message, we have a game customer and for the game industry we're talking about a thousand messages a second
which are flowing through the stream.
That's very fast. That's very fast.
Well, I think this is a very interesting architecture. We're able to use the cloud to help support edge networking.
I'd like to thank you for taking the time to share this architecture with us today.
And I would like to thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
